axis_descriptions.entity: Extract every important person/character in the range once each, merging recurrences into one canonical entry. Include foreground people/characters, speakers, addressed people/characters, partially visible but important interactors, and entities needed to understand actions, conflicts, portrayals, or relationships. Do not skip an unknown-name entity if it is distinguishable or narratively important. Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. Appearance segmentation — entities are extracted segment by segment, so a lead who recurs in every segment can crowd out the people around them. Treat each NEW person/character's first appearance as the start of a new entity segment. When several people appear at once, split that window into shorter appearance intervals and emit EVERY person in it — including secondary, briefly-seen, or interacting-but-not-central people — not only the most prominent one.
axis_descriptions.shot: This is the 1st of 13 parts of the video, and it is 294 seconds long. Segment this part into granular, contiguous shots using visual and audio cues: cuts, major camera-motion changes, event/action beats, dialogue or speaker turns, subject/framing/setting changes, and entity entries/exits. When unsure, prefer shorter coherent shots. Cover this part end to end with no gaps: the FIRST shot must start at 0 and the LAST shot must end at 294 (the full length of this part). Do not omit or trim static or silent spans at the head or tail.
axis_descriptions.video: This is the 1st of 13 parts of the video. Emit exactly ONE segment covering this whole part, using chunk-relative times: `start_time=0`, `end_time=294`.

segment_definitions.description: Extract every important person/character in the range once each, merging recurrences into one canonical entry. Include foreground people/characters, speakers, addressed people/characters, partially visible but important interactors, and entities needed to understand actions, conflicts, portrayals, or relationships. Do not skip an unknown-name entity if it is distinguishable or narratively important. Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. Appearance segmentation — entities are extracted segment by segment, so a lead who recurs in every segment can crowd out the people around them. Treat each NEW person/character's first appearance as the start of a new entity segment. When several people appear at once, split that window into shorter appearance intervals and emit EVERY person in it — including secondary, briefly-seen, or interacting-but-not-central people — not only the most prominent one.

fields.name: canonical_name
fields.description: Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. These entities are later merged across videos by matching name, so the name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'), which would wrongly merge unrelated people. One name per entity; the description elaborates further.

fields.name: entity_type
fields.description: Whether this entity is a person or a character.

fields.name: description
fields.description: Identifying description of this entity. Elaborate the entity to support its name and future cross-video merging: stable appearance, role, key relationships/interactions, and any grounded aliases or names heard. Prefer visible, spoken, OCR, or credited evidence over guesses.

fields.name: relationships
fields.description: Each relationship starting FROM this entity, output exactly as `dst_name;dst_type;rel_type;desc` (empty array if none). dst_name / dst_type must copy another emitted entity verbatim. rel_type is `actor_portrays_character` (person -> character only; desc must be `other`) or `has_social_relationship` (same type only). desc must be exactly one of family / friend / colleague / acquaintance / rival / other. Do not add evidence text, labels, colons, extra semicolons, or any value outside the enum in this field. Put relationship evidence in the entity description instead. Emit only clearly evidenced ties, not mere co-presence; emit symmetric social ties once.

fields.name: best_thumbnail
fields.description: Best, clearest, most front-facing frame of the entity. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

fields.name: second_best_thumbnail
fields.description: A frame as different as possible from best_thumbnail — a different time, ideally a different shot, and a different pose / angle / expression. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

fields.name: third_best_thumbnail
fields.description: A frame distinct from and far in time from both other thumbnails, so the three span the entity's varied appearances. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

segment_definitions.description: This is the 1st of 13 parts of the video, and it is 294 seconds long. Segment this part into granular, contiguous shots using visual and audio cues: cuts, major camera-motion changes, event/action beats, dialogue or speaker turns, subject/framing/setting changes, and entity entries/exits. When unsure, prefer shorter coherent shots. Cover this part end to end with no gaps: the FIRST shot must start at 0 and the LAST shot must end at 294 (the full length of this part). Do not omit or trim static or silent spans at the head or tail.

fields.name: description
fields.description: Describe this shot in rich, verbose detail — what happens in the story here (intent, action, consequence) and every visual and non-visual detail: the interactions, objects, setting, on-screen text, and a verbatim transcription of any speech (with the speaker when certain). Refer to each person or character by the EXACT same name you give it in the `entities` field. Write enough that the shot stands on its own and, read in order, the shots reconstruct the whole story.

fields.name: shot_type
fields.description: The type of the shot.

fields.name: camera_motion
fields.description: The camera motion of the shot.

fields.name: camera_angle
fields.description: The camera angle of the shot.

fields.name: entities
fields.description: Each entity evidenced in this shot, output exactly as `name;entity_type;description`. Include only entities visible, audible, or OCR-referenced in this shot: foreground or speaking persons/characters, important addressed or interacted-with people/characters, and entities needed to understand the action, conflict, portrayal, or relationship; also central places, objects, concepts, or things when they drive the shot's meaning. entity_type is one of person / character / place / object / concept / thing, lowercase. Use exactly two semicolons as separators; do not put semicolons inside name, entity_type, or description (use commas or dashes). For the `name` element (person/character): Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. Decide from all available context, not this shot alone. The name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'). **Use that entity's exact same name in every shot.** For the `description` element: Describe the entity for IDENTIFICATION — so that, from descriptions alone, one can tell which mentions across shots are the SAME person. Write SEVERAL sentences and pack in AS MANY stable identifying properties as you can: any name or alias heard, role, gender, approximate age, ethnicity, hair (color/length/style), facial features, build/height, distinctive marks, and especially their relationship or recurring interaction with other identified entities (e.g. 'the protagonist's mother', 'the man who repeatedly argues with the bald guard'), which strongly anchors identity. Do NOT describe clothing, location, or momentary physical action — they change and do not help identification.

segment_definitions.description: This is the 1st of 13 parts of the video. Emit exactly ONE segment covering this whole part, using chunk-relative times: `start_time=0`, `end_time=294`.

fields.name: title
fields.description: Title of the whole video; best guess from in-chunk evidence (titles / credits / dialogue).

fields.name: description
fields.description: Narrate this chunk's story in full using only evidence from the emitted shot segments — what happens and why: the plot progression, the characters who drive it and their motivations, the conflicts, turns, and consequences. Hold as much grounded information as possible; do not introduce unsupported facts. Integrate events, objects, story context, visual detail, and audio/OCR/transcription facts into one account: names, relationships, intentions, conflicts, interaction evidence, and production cues like 'Action!'/'Cut!' when evidenced. Transcribe spoken lines verbatim when audible; do not assert an uncertain speaker.

fields.name: work
fields.description: Optional work title inferred from this chunk (for root candidate materialization).

metadata.title: Technical Architecture Discussion and Mini-Tech Summit 2025
metadata.description: Angelo Rivera, a Fullstack Engineer, leads a video conference discussion about technical architecture, specifically debating the use of Postgres versus a namespace graph for online analytical processing. He emphasizes treating the challenge as a data engineering problem and proposes reorganizing the server-side epic around questions from the 'Mini-Tech Summit 2025.' During the call, Angelo experiences significant technical difficulties as his screen repeatedly freezes and goes black, prompting him to apologize and briefly leave the frame. Another participant, Omar Qansul, offers to share the document to assist. The video concludes with a screen share of a Google Doc titled 'Mini-Tech Summit 2025,' which outlines various technical topics and questions for the team to address.

gemini_result.title: Engineering Team Meeting on Knowledge Graph Architecture
gemini_result.description: A video recording of a Zoom meeting between two engineers, Angelo Ricco and Omar Qatout. Angelo discusses the architecture for a new service, comparing PostgreSQL to a namespace graph approach. He mentions discussing this with colleagues Nitin and Dimitri. He proposes organizing their work around unanswered questions from a 'Mini-Tech Summit 2025' document to build a 'knowledge graph as a service'. During the meeting, Angelo experiences technical difficulties with his computer freezing, prompting Omar to take over sharing the relevant Google Doc.

person_entities.description: A man with short hair, a beard, glasses, wearing a black t-shirt and a gold chain. His name is displayed as 'Angelo Ricco - Staff ML Engineer'.
A bald man with a beard, wearing a white t-shirt and black over-ear headphones. His name is displayed as 'Omar Qatout'.
A colleague mentioned by Angelo during the meeting.

shots.description: Angelo Ricco is speaking in a video call. He is wearing a black t-shirt, glasses, and a gold chain. He discusses the differences between online analytical processing using PostgreSQL versus a namespace graph. He mentions talking to Nitin and others about the benefits of a graph representation. He suggests treating it as a data engineering problem to avoid creating hundreds of MCP tools. He then notices his screen share has stopped.

person_entities.description: A man with short hair, a beard, glasses, wearing a black t-shirt and a gold chain. His name is displayed as 'Angelo Ricco - Staff ML Engineer'.

shots.narrative_descriptor: Angelo discusses online analytical processing, PostgreSQL, and namespace graphs. He mentions talking to Nitin. He asks if his screen share stopped.

shots.description: The view cuts to Omar Qatout, who is wearing a white t-shirt and black headphones. He confirms that Angelo's screen share is no longer visible.

person_entities.description: A bald man with a beard, wearing a white t-shirt and black over-ear headphones. His name is displayed as 'Omar Qatout'.

shots.narrative_descriptor: Omar confirms the screen share is gone.

shots.description: The view cuts back to Angelo. He mentions doing some reading over the weekend and sending links to Dimitri. He discusses code indexing architecture and SDLC architecture. He proposes going through the server-side epic and organizing it around unanswered questions from the Mini Tech Summit to build a good knowledge graph as a service. During this time, his computer screen freezes and his keyboard stops working, causing him to lean in and try to fix the issue.

person_entities.description: A man with short hair, a beard, glasses, wearing a black t-shirt and a gold chain. His name is displayed as 'Angelo Ricco - Staff ML Engineer'.

shots.description: The screen share ends, and Angelo is visible again. He emphasizes the need to answer the questions from the document in a cohesive way so that anyone can deploy the knowledge graph service. His screen freezes again.

metadata.title: Mini-Tech Summit 2025 Discussion
metadata.description: In this segment, Angelo Rivera leads a technical discussion during a video conference, sharing his screen to display a Google Doc titled 'Mini-Tech Summit 2025'. He reviews a list of open questions regarding service architecture, languages, and production readiness, emphasizing the need to address logging and observability as a dedicated epic before deployment. He suggests revisiting health checks and backup requirements. Angelo then proposes using Neo4j as a database alternative to Kuzu, citing its ability to handle federated queries and composite databases. He switches his screen share to the Neo4j documentation page titled 'Setting up and using a composite database' to illustrate the concept, asking his colleagues for their thoughts on integrating it into their Helm chart.

gemini_result.description: A recording of a Zoom meeting where an engineering team discusses project priorities, breaking down tasks into epics, and evaluating database architecture options, specifically comparing Kuzu and Neo4j.

shots.description: Angelo Brown is sharing his screen, displaying a Google Doc titled 'Background Info'. He scrolls through the document, discussing project priorities and the need to break down tasks into specific epics, such as logging, observability, SLAs, instrumentation, and indexing. He then transitions to discussing database architecture, comparing their current approach with Kuzu to the potential use of Neo4j, specifically mentioning a feature for composite databases. He searches for a link to the Neo4j documentation and shares it in the Zoom chat, confirming with Omar Safar that it has been sent. Angelo and Omar are visible in the Zoom participant gallery in the top right corner.

person_entities.description: The main speaker in the meeting, presenting his screen and leading the discussion on project priorities and database choices.

shots.narrative_descriptor: Angelo discusses project priorities, breaking down tasks into epics for logging, observability, and indexing. He then talks about database choices, comparing Kuzu and Neo4j, and mentions a Neo4j feature for composite databases. He says he will share a link in the chat. Angelo says: 'So we're all gonna like basically split it up... I think logging should be its own epic... observability, this needs its own epic... now that we have this segmentation strategy where we're going K8s native... it doesn't necessarily limit us... from using something like Neo4j as the database... let me send this to you guys if you want to pull it up on your screen Omar.' Omar replies: 'Yeah, it's in the chat?' Angelo confirms: 'Yeah I put it in the Zoom chat.'

shots.description: The screen share switches from the Google Doc to a Neo4j documentation page titled 'Setting up and using a composite database'. Angelo scrolls through the page, showing diagrams that illustrate how federated databases work within Neo4j. He asks Dimitri for his thoughts on potentially adding a Neo4j instance to their Helm chart. Dimitri unmutes and asks a clarifying question about how Neo4j can be used. Angelo and Omar remain visible in the participant gallery.

shots.narrative_descriptor: Angelo shows a Neo4j documentation page about composite databases and asks Dimitri for his thoughts on using this approach. Dimitri asks if it can be used similarly to SQLite. Angelo asks: 'I don't know what do you think Dimitri is that like before we even go there is that like a crazy idea or do you see any blockers or issues with it?' Dimitri responds: 'So can it be used kind of like SQLite?'

metadata.title: Neo4j Integration and Tenant Isolation Discussion
metadata.description: In this segment, a group of developers discusses the architectural implications of integrating a graph database server, specifically Neo4j, into their Kubernetes environment. The conversation begins with a debate on whether the database should be a proper server within the same pod or external, similar to a shared PostgreSQL instance. The speakers highlight the challenges of managing stateful services, emphasizing the need for proper backup, monitoring, and the distinction between mission-critical services and independent databases like Kuzu. One speaker suggests treating the integration as a data engineering problem, focusing on the ability to easily repopulate the database if issues arise. The discussion then shifts to Neo4j's capabilities, particularly its ability to provide tenant isolation through separate databases created via Cypher queries. The speakers visualize Neo4j as a separate container running behind the knowledge graph indexer, serving multiple services without the need for manual connection handling, contrasting this with the Kuzu approach.

gemini_result.title: Neo4j Documentation Review and Database Architecture Discussion
gemini_result.description: A screen recording of a video conference call where two men are discussing graph database architecture while reviewing Neo4j documentation. They debate the pros and cons of using a single database server versus independent databases, focusing on issues like deployment in Kubernetes, backup, monitoring, and tenant isolation. They compare Neo4j's capabilities with Kuzu, specifically regarding how to achieve data isolation for different projects.

shots.narrative_descriptor: The screen shows the Neo4j documentation page 'Insert data to your databases'. Two men in a video call discuss the architecture of a graph database server, debating whether to deploy it within a pod or outside Kubernetes like Postgres.

shots.description: The user navigates to the 'Setting up and using a composite database' page in the Neo4j documentation. The page explains how to use composite databases to access multiple graphs. The discussion continues, focusing on the challenges of managing a stateful service like a database. One speaker points out that a single database instance requires proper backup and monitoring, making it a tricky problem to solve. They contrast this with independent databases like Kuzu, which can be easily rebuilt and are less mission-critical. They also mention that even if Neo4j is external to the Kubernetes cluster, it can still be rebuilt from code.

person_entities.description: A man with dark hair and a beard, wearing a black t-shirt, sitting in a room with a window and a plant. He is a participant in the video call.
A man with short hair, wearing a headset, sitting in a room with a white wall. He is a participant in the video call.

thing_entities.description: The official documentation website for the Neo4j graph database, displayed in a web browser.

shots.narrative_descriptor: The user navigates to the 'Setting up and using a composite database' page. The men discuss the challenges of managing a stateful database service, comparing a single instance requiring backup and monitoring to independent databases like Kuzu that can be easily rebuilt.

shots.description: The user scrolls down the page to the 'Model your data for Composite database use' section. This section features diagrams illustrating a federated data model, showing how data from different databases (Product catalog and Customer Orders) can be combined. The conversation shifts to the topic of data isolation. One speaker explains that Kuzu was chosen because it provides proper isolation for each tenant at the project level, preventing unauthorized access to other graph databases. However, they note that Neo4j can also achieve this isolation by using the `CREATE DATABASE` command to create separate, isolated databases within the same server. They envision running Neo4j in a container behind a knowledge graph indexer, serving multiple databases similarly to Postgres.

fields.name: camera_motion
fields.description: The camera motion of the shot.

fields.name: camera_angle
fields.description: The camera angle of the shot.

fields.name: entities
fields.description: Each entity evidenced in this shot, output exactly as `name;entity_type;description`. Include only entities visible, audible, or OCR-referenced in this shot: foreground or speaking persons/characters, important addressed or interacted-with people/characters, and entities needed to understand the action, conflict, portrayal, or relationship; also central places, objects, concepts, or things when they drive the shot's meaning. entity_type is one of person / character / place / object / concept / thing, lowercase. Use exactly two semicolons as separators; do not put semicolons inside name, entity_type, or description (use commas or dashes). For the `name` element (person/character): Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. Decide from all available context, not this shot alone. The name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'). **Use that entity's exact same name in every shot.** For the `description` element: Describe the entity for IDENTIFICATION — so that, from descriptions alone, one can tell which mentions across shots are the SAME person. Write SEVERAL sentences and pack in AS MANY stable identifying properties as you can: any name or alias heard, role, gender, approximate age, ethnicity, hair (color/length/style), facial features, build/height, distinctive marks, and especially their relationship or recurring interaction with other identified entities (e.g. 'the protagonist's mother', 'the man who repeatedly argues with the bald guard'), which strongly anchors identity. Do NOT describe clothing, location, or momentary physical action — they change and do not help identification.

segment_definitions.description: This is the 4th of 13 parts of the video. Emit exactly ONE segment covering this whole part, using chunk-relative times: `start_time=0`, `end_time=294`.

fields.name: title
fields.description: Title of the whole video; best guess from in-chunk evidence (titles / credits / dialogue).

fields.name: description
fields.description: Narrate this chunk's story in full using only evidence from the emitted shot segments — what happens and why: the plot progression, the characters who drive it and their motivations, the conflicts, turns, and consequences. Hold as much grounded information as possible; do not introduce unsupported facts. Integrate events, objects, story context, visual detail, and audio/OCR/transcription facts into one account: names, relationships, intentions, conflicts, interaction evidence, and production cues like 'Action!'/'Cut!' when evidenced. Transcribe spoken lines verbatim when audible; do not assert an uncertain speaker.

fields.name: work
fields.description: Optional work title inferred from this chunk (for root candidate materialization).

params.summary_prompt: Summarize the video for segmentation context.

metadata.description: A man with short dark hair and a beard, wearing a headset, visible in the top-right corner of the video call. He is one of the speakers in the discussion.
A man with short dark hair, glasses, and a beard, wearing a headset, visible in the top-right corner of the video call. He is the primary speaker for most of the video.

The video shows a screen recording of the Neo4j Operations Manual webpage, specifically the section on 'Setting up and using a composite database'. The page displays diagrams illustrating 'Data Federation' and 'Data Sharing'. In the top right corner, a small video feed shows a man with glasses and a beard, identified as Angelo Rivera, who is speaking. He discusses the possibility of using a Neo4j Rust client to connect to a container and creating a new database for each project ID, contrasting this with the current Kuzu implementation. He raises concerns about performance and indexing when dealing with a large number of projects in a single graph database.
entities: Angelo Rivera; person; A man with glasses and a beard, visible in a small video feed in the top right corner. He is actively speaking and gesturing throughout the shot, discussing database architecture and performance considerations.

metadata.description: The screen recording continues to show the Neo4j Operations Manual webpage. The view scrolls down past the 'Data Sharing' section to 'Create databases for the composite', 'Import data to your databases', and 'Configure a Composite database'. Angelo Rivera continues to speak, suggesting that customers might prefer a server-based Neo4j solution over embedding Kuzu in their codebase. He emphasizes that the team is not married to any specific solution and should coordinate with the data engineering team. He then instructs the viewer to scroll down to see a specific query.
entities: Angelo Rivera; person; A man with glasses and a beard, visible in a small video feed in the top right corner. He continues his discussion on database solutions, advocating for flexibility and collaboration with the data engineering team.

metadata.description: The screen recording scrolls further down the Neo4j Operations Manual to the section 'Retrieve data with a single Cypher query', specifically focusing on 'Query across multiple shards'. Angelo Rivera expresses excitement about this feature, highlighting how Neo4j allows querying multiple databases with a single query using a 'proxy node'. He explains this concept with an example of sharding data by region for regulatory compliance and data isolation. He discusses the potential for creating a new database per project and the need to investigate Neo4j's scalability for this use case.
entities: Angelo Rivera; person; A man with glasses and a beard, visible in a small video feed in the top right corner. He is enthusiastic about the Neo4j feature of querying multiple shards with a single Cypher query, explaining its relevance to their use case of data isolation and project-specific databases.

metadata.title: Neo4j Composite Database Discussion
metadata.description: In this segment, two speakers discuss the technical architecture of a knowledge graph system, debating the merits of using Kuzu versus Neo4j. The screen displays the Neo4j Operations Manual, specifically the section on 'Setting up and using a composite database.' The speakers explore the concept of creating separate databases for different projects to handle large-scale data, such as 500,000 projects, and discuss performance implications. They highlight Neo4j's ability to query across multiple shards using a single Cypher query, a feature they find particularly useful for their use case. The conversation covers data isolation, physical node distribution, and the potential for scaling Neo4j to support a database-per-project model, emphasizing the need for further investigation into Neo4j's scalability limits.

gemini_result.title: Neo4j Operations Manual Discussion
gemini_result.description: A screen recording of a video call where two participants, Omar Qattan and Angus Binnie, are discussing database architecture using the Neo4j Operations Manual as a reference. They discuss the pros and cons of using Neo4j versus Kuzu, specifically focusing on how to handle multiple projects and data sharding. Angus explains how Neo4j's composite databases allow querying across multiple shards, which could solve their problem of managing numerous project-specific databases.

person_entities.description: A male participant in the video call, visible in the left video feed in the top right corner.
A male participant in the video call, visible in the right video feed in the top right corner. His label includes '- A.I.'.
A person mentioned by Angus Binnie during the discussion about Twitter's data sharding.

thing_entities.description: A graph database management system being discussed and whose documentation is shown on screen.
An embedded graph database mentioned as an alternative or current solution.
A financial services company mentioned as a hypothetical customer with many projects.
A social media company mentioned as an example of using regional data sharding.

shots.description: The screen displays the Neo4j Operations Manual, specifically a section titled 'Data Federation' with a diagram showing 'Product catalog' and 'Customer Orders' databases connecting to a 'Fabric database'. In the top right corner, there are two small video feeds showing Omar Qattan and Angus Binnie. Omar is speaking initially, then Angus takes over. They are discussing database connections and architecture. Angus compares their current approach with Kuzu to how it could be done in Neo4j, mentioning creating a database per project ID. He raises a concern about putting 500,000 projects into a single graph database for a large customer like UBS. Omar asks about customers wanting custom solutions versus using Neo4j as a service. The screen remains static on the documentation page throughout this shot.

person_entities.description: A male participant in the video call, visible in the left video feed in the top right corner.
A male participant in the video call, visible in the right video feed in the top right corner. His label includes '- A.I.'.

thing_entities.description: A graph database management system being discussed and whose documentation is shown on screen.
An embedded graph database mentioned as an alternative or current solution.
A financial services company mentioned as a hypothetical customer with many projects.

shots.narrative_descriptor: Omar Qattan and Angus Binnie discuss database architecture. Angus explains how they could use Neo4j similarly to Kuzu by creating a database per project ID. He says, 'instead of creating a new Kuzu database file, you just create a new, you run create database for project, for the project ID.' He then raises a scaling issue, mentioning a hypothetical scenario: 'let's say you have, you're a customer like UBS, and you have 500,000 projects. Putting 500,000 projects and all of the parsed code nodes and edges inside of one graph database might be possible, but should you do that? I don't know.' Omar asks about customer preferences for managed services.

shots.description: Angus instructs the person sharing the screen to scroll down. The screen scrolls down through the Neo4j Operations Manual, passing sections like 'Create databases for the composite' and 'Configure a Composite database'. The scrolling stops when the section 'Query across multiple shards' comes into view. The video feeds of Omar and Angus remain in the top right corner.

person_entities.description: A male participant in the video call, visible in the left video feed in the top right corner.
A male participant in the video call, visible in the right video feed in the top right corner. His label includes '- A.I.'.

thing_entities.description: A graph database management system being discussed and whose documentation is shown on screen.

shots.narrative_descriptor: Angus Binnie directs the navigation of the documentation. He says, 'scroll down actually. Keep scrolling. I just want you guys to see the query. Once you see the query you're gonna be like aha.' The screen scrolls down to the 'Query across multiple shards' section.

shots.description: The screen is zoomed in on the 'Query across multiple shards' section of the Neo4j manual. A Cypher query is visible, showing the use of 'USE composite.customers.EU' and 'USE composite.customers.US'. Angus explains the concept of querying multiple databases with a single query using a proxy node. He uses Twitter as an example of sharding data by region. Omar asks a clarifying question about the physical location of the nodes. Angus confirms they can be on separate file regions as long as they connect to the server. Angus concludes that this feature enables their specific use case.

person_entities.description: A male participant in the video call, visible in the left video feed in the top right corner.
A male participant in the video call, visible in the right video feed in the top right corner. His label includes '- A.I.'.
A person mentioned by Angus Binnie during the discussion about Twitter's data sharding.

thing_entities.description: A graph database management system being discussed and whose documentation is shown on screen.
A social media company mentioned as an example of using regional data sharding.

shots.narrative_descriptor: Angus Binnie explains the Cypher query shown on screen. He says, 'So you can create two different databases and query them with a single query. And you can do that across multiple databases. And so they have this concept called a proxy node.' He gives an example: 'if you have a regulation, this is very popular apparently, like Twitter apparently did this when I talked to Nitin, you need to shard data by your, like the region. And that's to provide isolation, like the data isolation.' Omar asks, 'Are they physically on, like they have to be physically on the same node or like they can be on different nodes?' Angus replies, 'I think they can be on, it's like as long as you're connecting to the Neo4j server, they can be on like literally separate file regions.'

axis_descriptions.entity: Extract every important person/character in the range once each, merging recurrences into one canonical entry. Include foreground people/characters, speakers, addressed people/characters, partially visible but important interactors, and entities needed to understand actions, conflicts, portrayals, or relationships. Do not skip an unknown-name entity if it is distinguishable or narratively important. Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. Appearance segmentation — entities are extracted segment by segment, so a lead who recurs in every segment can crowd out the people around them. Treat each NEW person/character's first appearance as the start of a new entity segment. When several people appear at once, split that window into shorter appearance intervals and emit EVERY person in it — including secondary, briefly-seen, or interacting-but-not-central people — not only the most prominent one.

When unsure, prefer shorter coherent shots. Cover this part end to end with no gaps: the FIRST shot must start at 0 and the LAST shot must end at 294 (the full length of this part). Emit only clearly evidenced ties, not mere co-presence; emit symmetric social ties once.

params.summary_prompt: Summarize the video for segmentation context.

metadata.description: A man with short brown hair, glasses, and a beard, wearing a black t-shirt. He is the primary speaker in the video, presenting a technical discussion about database architecture while sharing his screen.
A man with short dark hair and a beard, wearing a white t-shirt. He appears in the top right corner of the screen during a video conference, participating in the technical discussion.

The video shows a screen recording of a Neo4j documentation page titled 'Setting up and using a composite database'. A man, identified as Angelo Rivera, is visible in a small window in the top right corner. He is speaking about a database architecture, referencing an array of databases and a proxy node. The screen displays Cypher code examples, including a 'UNWIND' command and a 'CALL' block, which Angelo uses to illustrate his points about federated queries and traversing graphs across different namespaces.
entities: Angelo Rivera; person; A man with short brown hair and glasses, wearing a dark shirt, visible in a small window in the top right corner of the screen. He is the primary speaker in this segment, explaining database concepts.

metadata.description: The screen recording of the Neo4j documentation remains static. Angelo Rivera continues to speak, but another person, identified as Omar, briefly appears in the top right corner window, replacing Angelo. Omar is wearing headphones and a dark shirt. The conversation shifts to the practical limitations of the proposed architecture, with Omar questioning the feasibility of creating so many databases and performing operations like 'UNWIND' at runtime. Angelo reappears briefly to acknowledge the scale of the problem, mentioning '500' databases.
entities: Angelo Rivera; person; A man with short brown hair and glasses, wearing a dark shirt, visible in a small window in the top right corner of the screen. He is the primary speaker in this segment, explaining database concepts.

Omar; person; A man with short dark hair, wearing headphones and a dark shirt, visible in a small window in the top right corner of the screen. He questions the feasibility of the proposed database architecture.

metadata.description: The screen recording of the Neo4j documentation remains unchanged. A third person, identified as Dmitry, appears in the top right corner window. Dmitry has short dark hair and is wearing a light-colored shirt. He agrees with the previous speaker that the current architecture is insufficient and that a completely different data engineering approach is needed. He suggests splitting the data layer outside of the services. Angelo reappears and agrees with Dmitry's assessment, referencing a previous conversation with someone named Nitin about not putting indexing logic in Postgres.
entities: Angelo Rivera; person; A man with short brown hair and glasses, wearing a dark shirt, visible in a small window in the top right corner of the screen. He is the primary speaker in this segment, explaining database concepts.

Dmitry; person; A man with short dark hair, wearing a light-colored shirt, visible in a small window in the top right corner of the screen. He argues for a different architectural approach to the data layer.

metadata.title: Database Architecture Discussion
metadata.description: In this segment, a speaker presents a technical proposal for a database architecture using a screen recording of the Neo4j Operations Manual. The proposal involves creating a proxy node (project ID) and using a federated query approach, similar to the 'UNWIND' command shown on screen, to traverse multiple project databases. The speaker suggests that each project could have its own database for code indexing, allowing for cross-namespace queries. Other participants in the discussion raise concerns about the runtime feasibility of creating and querying hundreds of databases simultaneously. The conversation shifts to the current architecture, noting that Kuzu databases are currently contained within pods and accessible only by Neo4j nodes, suggesting that a significant architectural change, such as splitting the data layer, would be required to implement the proposed solution.

gemini_result.title: Neo4j Composite Databases Discussion
gemini_result.description: A video conference call where participants discuss the use of Neo4j composite databases for their architecture. The main speaker explains how they could use a proxy node and multiple databases for different projects, and how federated queries would work. Another participant joins the discussion to talk about the data engineering challenges and architectural limitations.

person_entities.description: Male participant in the video call, wearing a black t-shirt, glasses, and a headset. He is the primary speaker.
Male participant in the video call, wearing a grey t-shirt and glasses. He speaks later in the video.

shots.description: The video shows a screen recording of a web browser displaying the Neo4j Operations Manual, specifically the section on 'Setting up and using a composite database'. In the top right corner, there are video feeds of two participants. The primary speaker, wearing a black t-shirt and headset, explains how they could use Neo4j's composite database feature. He scrolls through the documentation, highlighting code snippets that show how to create a composite database and run queries across multiple databases. He discusses creating a proxy node and having separate databases for different projects, mentioning GitLab data like epics, issues, and vulnerabilities. He explains how federated queries would work to traverse the entire graph across different namespaces. Later, the second participant, wearing a grey t-shirt, chimes in to discuss the architectural implications, noting that their current setup with Kuzu databases is different and that this approach presents a new data engineering problem. They discuss the limitations of putting indexing logic in Postgres and the potential need for a separate data layer.

person_entities.description: Male participant in the video call, wearing a black t-shirt, glasses, and a headset. He is the primary speaker.
Male participant in the video call, wearing a grey t-shirt and glasses. He speaks later in the video.

shots.narrative_descriptor: A screen recording of a video call discussing Neo4j composite databases. The speaker explains how to use them for federated queries across multiple project databases, referencing GitLab data. Another participant discusses the architectural challenges and differences from their current Kuzu setup.

The video shows a screen recording of a web browser displaying the Neo4j Operations Manual. The page is focused on a tutorial about setting up and using a composite database, with a code snippet visible. In the top right corner, a small video feed shows a man with glasses and a beard, identified as Angelo Rivera, speaking. He discusses the benefits of splitting the data layer and mentions a tool called Siphon. He talks about meeting with Dennis Tang to get more information on the state of Siphon. The conversation shifts to the trade-offs of using Neo4j and why it wasn't used initially, citing Cypher queries and segmentation strategy as reasons. Angelo emphasizes the importance of doing things right rather than fast, and mentions the Mini-Tech Summit as a potential opportunity to get answers about Siphon.

Angelo Rivera continues to speak, explaining that Siphon reads from the logical replication stream of PostgreSQL and feeds it to a NATS Jetstream. metadata.title: Data Indexing Strategy: Neo4j and Siphon Discussion
metadata.description: In this segment, a team discusses architectural strategies for data indexing, focusing on Neo4j and a tool called Siphon. The conversation begins with a speaker explaining that Siphon subscribes to a service rather than the primary database, avoiding direct interaction with the primary. Another speaker highlights the benefits of splitting the data layer, referencing their work with Zook and the need to index code across different replicas for redundancy. The team debates the trade-offs of using Neo4j, recalling that they initially avoided it due to the complexity of generating Cypher queries and spinning up stateful services without a clear segmentation strategy. They agree to continue building the basic plumbing for indexing SDLC data, prioritizing correctness over speed, with a target timeline extending to the Mini Tech Summit. The discussion concludes with a technical explanation of Siphon, which reads from a Postgres logical replication stream and feeds data into a NATS Jetstream for consumption, as the screen transitions to the Siphon GitHub repository.

Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. metadata.title: Siphon vs. Postgres Triggers for SDLC Indexing
metadata.description: In this segment, the team discusses the feasibility of using the 'Siphon' tool for SDLC indexing versus using Postgres triggers. The conversation centers on the technical and procedural hurdles of deploying Siphon, including the need for a readiness review and approval from the database team to connect to the replication stream. Dimitri provides context that the security infrastructure team previously opted for Postgres triggers because Siphon was not ready for self-managed customers. The team debates the performance implications of triggers on the primary instance versus the benefits of Siphon's replication stream. They also explore the architectural implications of tying SDLC indexing to the code graph, suggesting the potential use of different graph databases like Kuzu for code and a separate solution for SDLC data.

gemini_result.title: Engineering Sync: Siphon, Postgres Triggers, and SDLC Indexing
gemini_result.description: A recording of a video conference call between two engineers, Angelo Bruno and Dmitry Stus. They are discussing the technical implementation details of a project called 'Siphon', which involves replicating data from a PostgreSQL database. The conversation covers the use of Pub/Sub, the readiness of Siphon for production, and the potential use of Postgres triggers as a temporary alternative. They also discuss the implications for SDLC indexing and the possibility of using different graph databases, such as Kuzu, for different namespaces. The screen displays a GitLab repository page for the Siphon project throughout the video.

shots.description: Dmitry Stus explains his perspective on Postgres triggers, describing them as a temporary solution while waiting for Siphon to be fully ready. He expresses concerns about the current deployment status of Siphon, noting it still needs to be connected to Postgres and deployed to production, which could delay their January timeline. He outlines the necessary processes, including a readiness review and obtaining approval from the database team. The screen share scrolls down to show the 'Getting started' and 'Prerequisites' sections of the Siphon README.

metadata.title: Technical Sync: Siphon Architecture and Knowledge Graph Naming
metadata.description: This segment captures a technical meeting where the team discusses architectural decisions and project status. Initially, the screen displays a GitHub repository page for 'Siphon,' a tool for replicating data from PostgreSQL. The team debates the complexity of the architecture and the value of SDLC items, with one member suggesting an iterative approach to keep components separate. Another participant highlights the benefit of using Kuzu on the frontend and suggests keeping it for code while re-evaluating the graph database for SDLC data. The view then switches to a Google Doc titled 'Notes - Knowledge Graph Sync,' dated 'Sep 29, 2025.' The discussion shifts to a naming issue regarding the 'Zook orchestrator,' which a participant finds confusing and proposes renaming to 'knowledge graph proxy.' The team agrees on the new name, and the speaker notes they will open a merge request to update the design document and code. The segment concludes with a status update on adding endpoints for indexing on the knowledge graph side.
metadata.work: Knowledge Graph Server Side Sync Meeting

Speaker 1 and Angelos discuss the architecture, specifically the complexity of connecting code to SDLC items. Speaker 1 suggests keeping Kuzu for code and re-evaluating the graph database for SDLC data, noting that Kuzu is already used on the frontend. Angelos agrees. Angelos then realizes they are over time and asks if they have time to look at the current epic.

shots.narrative_descriptor: Speaker 1 says connecting the code to SDLC items could complicate the architecture. Angelos agrees, suggesting they keep them separate for now and manually stitch them together. Speaker 1 adds that a benefit of Kuzu is that it's already used on the frontend, allowing them to transfer work to the server. He suggests keeping Kuzu for code and re-evaluating the graph database for SDLC data. Angelos agrees. Speaker 1 concludes his points. Angelos asks if they have time to look at the current epic and switches to a Google Doc.

shots.description: The screen switches to a Google Doc titled 'Knowledge Graph Server Sync'. Angelos scrolls through the document. Speaker 1 mentions he can stay longer and brings up a concern from Jason about the name 'Zokt orchestrator' being confusing. Speaker 1 suggests renaming it to 'Knowledge Graph Proxy'. Angelos asks Dimitri for his opinion. Dimitri agrees with 'proxy', suggesting 'gkg-proxy', and notes that the helm chart name is more important than the code name. Speaker 1 mentions he is already adding it to the GDK and is blocked by the name choice. He asks Angelos for final approval, which Angelos gives. Speaker 1 then provides a status update on adding endpoints for indexing.

shots.narrative_descriptor: Angelos switches to a Google Doc titled 'Knowledge Graph Server Sync' and scrolls through it. Speaker 1 says he can stay longer and brings up a concern from Jason about the name 'Zokt orchestrator' being confusing. Speaker 1 agrees with Jason and suggests 'Knowledge Graph Proxy' instead. Angelos asks Dimitri for his opinion. Dimitri agrees with 'proxy', suggesting 'gkg-proxy'. Speaker 1 mentions he's already using that name in the GDK and is blocked by the name choice. He says he will open an MR for the design document and reflect the change in the code. Speaker 1 asks Angelos what he thinks. Angelos agrees. Speaker 1 gives a status update, saying he will start adding endpoints for indexing on the knowledge graph side later in the week.

metadata.title: Knowledge Graph Server Side Sync - Part 9
metadata.description: In this segment, a team discusses the architecture and responsibilities for a Knowledge Graph Server Side Sync project. Angelo Rivera proposes splitting the workload, taking on the querying side which involves querying Kuzu and using GitLab's API (GitLab client) to read files and extract code chunks on the server side to avoid performance issues. He emphasizes the need for observability, defining SLAs/SLOs for indexing, error reporting, and ensuring reliability so that indexing failures don't take down the service. He also mentions the need for proper logging and memory handling. Omar Qutad expresses a desire for engineers to have better access to infrastructure, specifically Kubernetes, to monitor running containers, referencing a past issue with Teleport. Dimitri Staff confirms he has Rails console access but notes that Kubernetes access has been unsuccessful to obtain.

gemini_result.title: Knowledge Graph Server Core Sync Meeting
gemini_result.description: A screen recording of a Google Meet call where three software engineers, Omar Qattan, Aayush Shrestha, and Dmitry Vovk, are discussing the architecture and work streams for a Knowledge Graph Server project. They are looking at a Google Doc outlining the agenda. Aayush proposes splitting the work, taking on the querying side involving Kuzu and a Gitaly Rust client, while suggesting others handle deployment and observability. Omar then brings up the topic of infrastructure access, expressing a desire for Kubernetes access for debugging, to which Dmitry responds that such access is currently restricted.

axis_descriptions.entity: Extract every important person/character in the range once each, merging recurrences into one canonical entry. Include foreground people/characters, speakers, addressed people/characters, partially visible but important interactors, and entities needed to understand actions, conflicts, portrayals, or relationships. Do not skip an unknown-name entity if it is distinguishable or narratively important. Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. Appearance segmentation — entities are extracted segment by segment, so a lead who recurs in every segment can crowd out the people around them. Treat each NEW person/character's first appearance as the start of a new entity segment. When several people appear at once, split that window into shorter appearance intervals and emit EVERY person in it — including secondary, briefly-seen, or interacting-but-not-central people — not only the most prominent one.

fields.name: relationships

Do not add evidence text, labels, colons, extra semicolons, or any value outside the enum in this field. Put relationship evidence in the entity description instead. Emit only clearly evidenced ties, not mere co-presence; emit symmetric social ties once.

fields.name: entities
fields.description: Each entity evidenced in this shot, output exactly as `name;entity_type;description`. Include only entities visible, audible, or OCR-referenced in this shot: foreground or speaking persons/characters, important addressed or interacted-with people/characters, and entities needed to understand the action, conflict, portrayal, or relationship; also central places, objects, concepts, or things when they drive the shot's meaning. entity_type is one of person / character / place / object / concept / thing, lowercase. Use exactly two semicolons as separators; do not put semicolons inside name, entity_type, or description (use commas or dashes). For the `name` element (person/character): Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. Decide from all available context, not this shot alone. The name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'). **Use that entity's exact same name in every shot.** For the `description` element: Describe the entity for IDENTIFICATION — so that, from descriptions alone, one can tell which mentions across shots are the SAME person. Write SEVERAL sentences and pack in AS MANY stable identifying properties as you can: any name or alias heard, role, gender, approximate age, ethnicity, hair (color/length/style), facial features, build/height, distinctive marks, and especially their relationship or recurring interaction with other identified entities (e.g. 'the protagonist's mother', 'the man who repeatedly argues with the bald guard'), which strongly anchors identity. Do NOT describe clothing, location, or momentary physical action — they change and do not help identification.

Integrate events, objects, story context, visual detail, and audio/OCR/transcription facts into one account: names, relationships, intentions, conflicts, interaction evidence, and production cues like 'Action!'/'Cut!' when evidenced. Transcribe spoken lines verbatim when audible; do not assert an uncertain speaker.

metadata.description: A man with short dark hair and a beard, wearing a headset, visible in the top-right corner of the video call. He is actively participating in the discussion about infrastructure access.
A man with short dark hair and glasses, visible in the top-right corner of the video call. He is leading the discussion and sharing his screen, which displays Google Docs.

The video shows a screen recording of a Google Docs document titled 'Notes - Knowledge Graph Sync'. Omar Quraishi is visible in a small window in the top right corner. The document contains meeting notes dated 'Sep 29, 2025' and 'Sep 22, 2025'. The team discusses access control issues, specifically regarding Rails console commands and kubectl access in staging and production environments. They mention that change requests with Rails console commands are not allowed anymore. Omar types a note into the document: 'Omar: Figure out access control to Staging both kubectl kb, and Rails Console.'
entities: Omar Quraishi; person; A man with short dark hair and a beard, wearing a headset, visible in the top right corner of the screen during a video conference. He is actively participating in the discussion and typing notes into a shared Google Doc.

metadata.description: The screen recording switches to a different Google Docs document titled 'Mini-Tech Summit 2025'. Angelo Rivera is now visible in the top right corner. The document outlines topics for a mini-tech summit, including 'Service Architecture', 'Languages & Frameworks', 'Production Readiness', and 'Operations'. Angelo discusses his plan to review the 'Knowledge Graph server epic' and ensure all questions are answered, mentioning observability and OpenTelemetry. He suggests assigning tasks to Bogdan or Jean-Gabriel. The team agrees to review the epic in another call to identify any missing issues.
entities: Angelo Rivera; person; A man with short dark hair and glasses, visible in the top right corner of the screen during a video conference. He is leading the discussion about the mini-tech summit and the Knowledge Graph server epic.

metadata.title: Knowledge Graph Sync and Mini-Tech Summit Planning
metadata.description: In this segment, a team discusses infrastructure access and project planning during a video conference. The screen displays a Google Doc titled 'Notes - Knowledge Graph Sync,' where Omar Qutad is seen typing a note about figuring out access control for the staging Kubernetes cluster and Rails console. The team debates the necessity of read access for staging environments, with one speaker expressing a willingness to 'fight people' for it. The conversation then shifts to a 'Mini-Tech Summit 2025' document, where a speaker outlines a plan to review an epic, assign tasks related to observability and OpenTelemetry to team members like Bogdan and Jean-Gabriel, and define metrics for the project. The discussion highlights the need to ensure all questions are answered and to speed up the process by creating specific issues for missing topics.

metadata.title: Mini-Tech Summit 2025 - Knowledge Graph Implementation Discussion
metadata.description: In this segment of the Mini-Tech Summit 2025, the team discusses technical implementation details for a knowledge graph system. The conversation begins with a review of container resource monitoring (disk and memory usage) and deployment tools like Helm charts and container images. A speaker notes that logs are already being ingested into Kibana for the 'zooked' service and suggests adding structured logging. The discussion then shifts to the core challenge of 'incremental indexing' and synchronizing knowledge graph updates with database connections. Mike is identified as a potential lead for this task, having prior context from work done before the switch to the service architecture. The team debates the current state of the re-indexing logic, with one speaker noting it is not fully functional. They propose a stopgap solution involving saving Parquet files for definitions and references. The conversation concludes with a technical clarification regarding Git's API, specifically that it reports file changes but not directory deletions or renames, which presents a challenge for the indexing logic that needs further clarification.

Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. Treat each NEW person/character's first appearance as the start of a new entity segment. The video shows a screen recording of a Google Docs document titled 'Mini-Tech Summit 2025'. The document contains a list of questions under the 'Background' section. In the top right corner, a small video feed shows a man with short dark hair and glasses, identified as Angelo Rivera, participating in a video conference. He is speaking about handling different types of changes, specifically mentioning directories and files, and the need to test server-side functionality. metadata.description: The screen recording switches to a different Google Docs document titled 'Notes - Knowledge Graph Sync'. The document contains meeting notes dated 'Sep 29, 2025' and 'Sep 22, 2025'. Angelo Rivera continues to speak, discussing the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) and the potential impact of a tool called 'Siphon' on their architecture. He questions whether code indexing tasks should come from Siphon or Postgres. Another participant, Dimitri, responds that Siphon subscribes to events from tables, and code indexing happens in Gitaly, involving push and merge events.

Angelo then navigates to a specific merge request page within GitLab.

metadata.description: The screen recording focuses on a GitLab merge request page titled 'Adding HTTP Support to Knowledge Graph Client'. The page shows code changes, comments, and pipeline status. Angelo Rivera asks about the data source for the merge request page, specifically whether it queries Rails or Gitaly. Dimitri confirms that the data is loaded from Gitaly. Angelo expresses surprise, as he thought the data came from Postgres. They discuss the ephemeral nature of push branch service events and the need to capture commit events for SDLC purposes.

metadata.title: Knowledge Graph Architecture and SDLC Discussion
metadata.description: In this segment, a team of developers, including Angelo Rivera, Omar Qureshi, and Dmitry Grudzi, are in a video conference discussing the architecture for a Knowledge Graph project. They are reviewing a Google Doc titled 'Mini-Tech Summit 2025' which outlines questions about service architecture, languages, and production readiness. The team discusses the complexities of handling directory changes and generating diffs from GitLab. They decide to split their efforts, with Angelo and Mike working on one aspect and Dmitry and Omar taking the querying side. The conversation shifts to the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) and whether code indexing tasks should come from Postgres or a new system called Siphon. They explore how GitLab stores events, specifically merge requests and commits, and whether this data is saved to a Postgres table or fetched directly from Gitaly. The team navigates through GitLab's interface, examining merge requests and commit histories to understand the data flow and storage mechanisms, concluding that while events are stored, the code itself is fetched from Gitaly.

gemini_result.description: A screen recording of a video conference call where three participants, Omar Qattan, Angelo Brown, and Dimitri, are discussing technical details related to a 'Knowledge Graph Server Sync'. The video primarily shows a screen share of a Google Doc containing meeting notes and various pages within the GitLab application, including merge request lists, a user dashboard, and specific code diffs. The participants discuss handling directory changes, fetching diffs from Gitaly, and whether commit events are stored in a Postgres database or loaded dynamically.

axis_descriptions.entity: Extract every important person/character in the range once each, merging recurrences into one canonical entry. Include foreground people/characters, speakers, addressed people/characters, partially visible but important interactors, and entities needed to understand actions, conflicts, portrayals, or relationships. Do not skip an unknown-name entity if it is distinguishable or narratively important. Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. Appearance segmentation — entities are extracted segment by segment, so a lead who recurs in every segment can crowd out the people around them. Treat each NEW person/character's first appearance as the start of a new entity segment. When several people appear at once, split that window into shorter appearance intervals and emit EVERY person in it — including secondary, briefly-seen, or interacting-but-not-central people — not only the most prominent one.
axis_descriptions.shot: This is the 13th of 13 parts of the video, and it is 294 seconds long. Segment this part into granular, contiguous shots using visual and audio cues: cuts, major camera-motion changes, event/action beats, dialogue or speaker turns, subject/framing/setting changes, and entity entries/exits. When unsure, prefer shorter coherent shots. Cover this part end to end with no gaps: the FIRST shot must start at 0 and the LAST shot must end at 294 (the full length of this part). Do not omit or trim static or silent spans at the head or tail.
axis_descriptions.video: This is the 13th of 13 parts of the video. Emit exactly ONE segment covering this whole part, using chunk-relative times: `start_time=0`, `end_time=294`.

segment_definitions.description: Extract every important person/character in the range once each, merging recurrences into one canonical entry. Do not skip an unknown-name entity if it is distinguishable or narratively important. Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. Appearance segmentation — entities are extracted segment by segment, so a lead who recurs in every segment can crowd out the people around them. Treat each NEW person/character's first appearance as the start of a new entity segment. When several people appear at once, split that window into shorter appearance intervals and emit EVERY person in it — including secondary, briefly-seen, or interacting-but-not-central people — not only the most prominent one.

fields.name: canonical_name
fields.description: Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. These entities are later merged across videos by matching name, so the name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'), which would wrongly merge unrelated people. One name per entity; the description elaborates further.

fields.name: entity_type
fields.description: Whether this entity is a person or a character.

fields.name: description
fields.description: Identifying description of this entity. Elaborate the entity to support its name and future cross-video merging: stable appearance, role, key relationships/interactions, and any grounded aliases or names heard. Prefer visible, spoken, OCR, or credited evidence over guesses.

fields.name: relationships
fields.description: Each relationship starting FROM this entity, output exactly as `dst_name;dst_type;rel_type;desc` (empty array if none). dst_name / dst_type must copy another emitted entity verbatim. rel_type is `actor_portrays_character` (person -> character only; desc must be `other`) or `has_social_relationship` (same type only). desc must be exactly one of family / friend / colleague / acquaintance / rival / other. Do not add evidence text, labels, colons, extra semicolons, or any value outside the enum in this field. Put relationship evidence in the entity description instead. Emit only clearly evidenced ties, not mere co-presence; emit symmetric social ties once.

fields.name: best_thumbnail
fields.description: Best, clearest, most front-facing frame of the entity. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

fields.name: second_best_thumbnail
fields.description: A frame as different as possible from best_thumbnail — a different time, ideally a different shot, and a different pose / angle / expression. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

fields.name: third_best_thumbnail
fields.description: A frame distinct from and far in time from both other thumbnails, so the three span the entity's varied appearances. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

Segment this part into granular, contiguous shots using visual and audio cues: cuts, major camera-motion changes, event/action beats, dialogue or speaker turns, subject/framing/setting changes, and entity entries/exits. When unsure, prefer shorter coherent shots. Cover this part end to end with no gaps: the FIRST shot must start at 0 and the LAST shot must end at 294 (the full length of this part). Do not omit or trim static or silent spans at the head or tail.

fields.name: description
fields.description: Describe this shot in rich, verbose detail — what happens in the story here (intent, action, consequence) and every visual and non-visual detail: the interactions, objects, setting, on-screen text, and a verbatim transcription of any speech (with the speaker when certain). Refer to each person or character by the EXACT same name you give it in the `entities` field. Write enough that the shot stands on its own and, read in order, the shots reconstruct the whole story.

fields.name: shot_type
fields.description: The type of the shot.

fields.name: camera_motion
fields.description: The camera motion of the shot.

fields.name: camera_angle
fields.description: The camera angle of the shot.

fields.name: entities
fields.description: Each entity evidenced in this shot, output exactly as `name;entity_type;description`. Include only entities visible, audible, or OCR-referenced in this shot: foreground or speaking persons/characters, important addressed or interacted-with people/characters, and entities needed to understand the action, conflict, portrayal, or relationship; also central places, objects, concepts, or things when they drive the shot's meaning. entity_type is one of person / character / place / object / concept / thing, lowercase. Use exactly two semicolons as separators; do not put semicolons inside name, entity_type, or description (use commas or dashes). For the `name` element (person/character): Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. Decide from all available context, not this shot alone. The name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'). **Use that entity's exact same name in every shot.** For the `description` element: Describe the entity for IDENTIFICATION — so that, from descriptions alone, one can tell which mentions across shots are the SAME person. Write SEVERAL sentences and pack in AS MANY stable identifying properties as you can: any name or alias heard, role, gender, approximate age, ethnicity, hair (color/length/style), facial features, build/height, distinctive marks, and especially their relationship or recurring interaction with other identified entities (e.g. 'the protagonist's mother', 'the man who repeatedly argues with the bald guard'), which strongly anchors identity. Do NOT describe clothing, location, or momentary physical action — they change and do not help identification.

metadata.title: Knowledge Graph Server Side Sync Meeting
metadata.description: The video captures a virtual team meeting focused on the 'Knowledge Graph Server Side Sync' project. It begins with a screen share of a Google Doc detailing meeting notes from September 22 and 29, 2025, outlining tasks for team members like Omar, Dmitry, and Angelo. The discussion then shifts to the participants' video feeds, where Angelo Rivera proposes centralizing the project's Epic under the 'Rust epics' category for better organization. Omar Qomi agrees, provided issues remain under their relevant projects like 'Zoekt'. The team discusses a 'Ruby mine' indexing problem, with Omar offering to investigate the logs. Angelo outlines a plan to centralize information and run two parallel streams of work: the current project and SDLC indexing, aiming to move the SDLC components by the end of the week. The meeting concludes with lighthearted banter about an upcoming trip to Frankfurt, where Angelo jokes about learning German to order beer, and Omar offers to teach him.

gemini_result.title: Engineering Team Sync Meeting
gemini_result.description: A recording of a video conference call between three software engineers: Angelo Masi, Omar Qatna, and Kamil Mroti. The video begins with a screen share of a Google Doc titled 'Knowledge Graph Server Sync Sync', where they discuss commit metadata and indexing. The view then switches to a gallery view of the three participants. They discuss centralizing project epics, renaming a project currently called 'Rust', and debugging an indexing issue related to 'Ruby mine'. Angelo outlines the next steps, including meetings with Dennis and Adam, creating a new design document, and prioritizing SDLC indexing. As the meeting concludes, Omar mentions his upcoming vacation and preparations for a trip to Frankfurt. Angelo expresses excitement about the trip and jokes about needing to learn how to order a beer in German, which Omar offers to teach him. The meeting ends with everyone saying goodbye.
