axis_descriptions.entity: Extract every important person/character in the range once each, merging recurrences into one canonical entry. Include foreground people/characters, speakers, addressed people/characters, partially visible but important interactors, and entities needed to understand actions, conflicts, portrayals, or relationships. Do not skip an unknown-name entity if it is distinguishable or narratively important. Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. Appearance segmentation — entities are extracted segment by segment, so a lead who recurs in every segment can crowd out the people around them. Treat each NEW person/character's first appearance as the start of a new entity segment. When several people appear at once, split that window into shorter appearance intervals and emit EVERY person in it — including secondary, briefly-seen, or interacting-but-not-central people — not only the most prominent one.
axis_descriptions.shot: This is the 5th of 10 parts of the video, and it is 297 seconds long. Segment this part into granular, contiguous shots using visual and audio cues: cuts, major camera-motion changes, event/action beats, dialogue or speaker turns, subject/framing/setting changes, and entity entries/exits. When unsure, prefer shorter coherent shots. Cover this part end to end with no gaps: the FIRST shot must start at 0 and the LAST shot must end at 297 (the full length of this part). Do not omit or trim static or silent spans at the head or tail.
axis_descriptions.video: This is the 5th of 10 parts of the video. Emit exactly ONE segment covering this whole part, using chunk-relative times: `start_time=0`, `end_time=297`.

segment_definitions.description: Extract every important person/character in the range once each, merging recurrences into one canonical entry. Include foreground people/characters, speakers, addressed people/characters, partially visible but important interactors, and entities needed to understand actions, conflicts, portrayals, or relationships. Do not skip an unknown-name entity if it is distinguishable or narratively important. Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. Appearance segmentation — entities are extracted segment by segment, so a lead who recurs in every segment can crowd out the people around them. Treat each NEW person/character's first appearance as the start of a new entity segment. When several people appear at once, split that window into shorter appearance intervals and emit EVERY person in it — including secondary, briefly-seen, or interacting-but-not-central people — not only the most prominent one.

fields.name: canonical_name
fields.description: Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. These entities are later merged across videos by matching name, so the name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'), which would wrongly merge unrelated people. One name per entity; the description elaborates further.

fields.name: entity_type
fields.description: Whether this entity is a person or a character.

fields.name: description
fields.description: Identifying description of this entity. Elaborate the entity to support its name and future cross-video merging: stable appearance, role, key relationships/interactions, and any grounded aliases or names heard. Prefer visible, spoken, OCR, or credited evidence over guesses.

fields.name: relationships
fields.description: Each relationship starting FROM this entity, output exactly as `dst_name;dst_type;rel_type;desc` (empty array if none). dst_name / dst_type must copy another emitted entity verbatim. rel_type is `actor_portrays_character` (person -> character only; desc must be `other`) or `has_social_relationship` (same type only). desc must be exactly one of family / friend / colleague / acquaintance / rival / other. Do not add evidence text, labels, colons, extra semicolons, or any value outside the enum in this field. Put relationship evidence in the entity description instead. Emit only clearly evidenced ties, not mere co-presence; emit symmetric social ties once.

fields.name: best_thumbnail
fields.description: Best, clearest, most front-facing frame of the entity. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

fields.name: second_best_thumbnail
fields.description: A frame as different as possible from best_thumbnail — a different time, ideally a different shot, and a different pose / angle / expression. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

fields.name: third_best_thumbnail
fields.description: A frame distinct from and far in time from both other thumbnails, so the three span the entity's varied appearances. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

segment_definitions.description: This is the 5th of 10 parts of the video, and it is 297 seconds long. Segment this part into granular, contiguous shots using visual and audio cues: cuts, major camera-motion changes, event/action beats, dialogue or speaker turns, subject/framing/setting changes, and entity entries/exits. When unsure, prefer shorter coherent shots. Cover this part end to end with no gaps: the FIRST shot must start at 0 and the LAST shot must end at 297 (the full length of this part). Do not omit or trim static or silent spans at the head or tail.

fields.name: description
fields.description: Describe this shot in rich, verbose detail — what happens in the story here (intent, action, consequence) and every visual and non-visual detail: the interactions, objects, setting, on-screen text, and a verbatim transcription of any speech (with the speaker when certain). Refer to each person or character by the EXACT same name you give it in the `entities` field. Write enough that the shot stands on its own and, read in order, the shots reconstruct the whole story.

fields.name: shot_type
fields.description: The type of the shot.

fields.name: camera_motion
fields.description: The camera motion of the shot.

fields.name: camera_angle
fields.description: The camera angle of the shot.

fields.name: entities
fields.description: Each entity evidenced in this shot, output exactly as `name;entity_type;description`. Include only entities visible, audible, or OCR-referenced in this shot: foreground or speaking persons/characters, important addressed or interacted-with people/characters, and entities needed to understand the action, conflict, portrayal, or relationship; also central places, objects, concepts, or things when they drive the shot's meaning. entity_type is one of person / character / place / object / concept / thing, lowercase. Use exactly two semicolons as separators; do not put semicolons inside name, entity_type, or description (use commas or dashes). For the `name` element (person/character): Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. Decide from all available context, not this shot alone. The name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'). **Use that entity's exact same name in every shot.** For the `description` element: Describe the entity for IDENTIFICATION — so that, from descriptions alone, one can tell which mentions across shots are the SAME person. Write SEVERAL sentences and pack in AS MANY stable identifying properties as you can: any name or alias heard, role, gender, approximate age, ethnicity, hair (color/length/style), facial features, build/height, distinctive marks, and especially their relationship or recurring interaction with other identified entities (e.g. 'the protagonist's mother', 'the man who repeatedly argues with the bald guard'), which strongly anchors identity. Do NOT describe clothing, location, or momentary physical action — they change and do not help identification.

segment_definitions.description: This is the 5th of 10 parts of the video. Emit exactly ONE segment covering this whole part, using chunk-relative times: `start_time=0`, `end_time=297`.

fields.name: title
fields.description: Title of the whole video; best guess from in-chunk evidence (titles / credits / dialogue).

fields.name: description
fields.description: Narrate this chunk's story in full using only evidence from the emitted shot segments — what happens and why: the plot progression, the characters who drive it and their motivations, the conflicts, turns, and consequences. Hold as much grounded information as possible; do not introduce unsupported facts. Integrate events, objects, story context, visual detail, and audio/OCR/transcription facts into one account: names, relationships, intentions, conflicts, interaction evidence, and production cues like 'Action!'/'Cut!' when evidenced. Transcribe spoken lines verbatim when audible; do not assert an uncertain speaker.

fields.name: work
fields.description: Optional work title inferred from this chunk (for root candidate materialization).

metadata.title: Southampton vs Liverpool - Match Highlights
metadata.description: In this segment of the Premier League match between Southampton and Liverpool, the score stands at 1-0 to Southampton. The play is dynamic, with both teams vying for control in the midfield. Liverpool's Thiago (No. 6) is highlighted, with commentary noting his limited playing time due to recent injuries and a red card incident against Everton. The action includes a throw-in by Thiago, a long pass from Alexander-Arnold that goes out of play, and a throw-in by Southampton's Bertrand. The commentators discuss the high pressing styles of both teams and the potential for a deal involving Theo Walcott. The segment captures the tactical battle and individual player moments, including close-ups of Thiago and Mohamed Salah, as the game progresses without a change in the scoreline.

axis_descriptions.entity: Extract every important person/character in the range once each, merging recurrences into one canonical entry. Include foreground people/characters, speakers, addressed people/characters, partially visible but important interactors, and entities needed to understand actions, conflicts, portrayals, or relationships. Do not skip an unknown-name entity if it is distinguishable or narratively important. Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. Appearance segmentation — entities are extracted segment by segment, so a lead who recurs in every segment can crowd out the people around them. Treat each NEW person/character's first appearance as the start of a new entity segment. When several people appear at once, split that window into shorter appearance intervals and emit EVERY person in it — including secondary, briefly-seen, or interacting-but-not-central people — not only the most prominent one.

Do not omit or trim static or silent spans at the head or tail.
axis_descriptions.video: This is the 6th of 10 parts of the video. Emit exactly ONE segment covering this whole part, using chunk-relative times: `start_time=0`, `end_time=296`.

fields.name: canonical_name
fields.description: Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. These entities are later merged across videos by matching name, so the name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'), which would wrongly merge unrelated people. One name per entity; the description elaborates further.

fields.name: entity_type
fields.description: Whether this entity is a person or a character.

fields.name: description
fields.description: Identifying description of this entity. Elaborate the entity to support its name and future cross-video merging: stable appearance, role, key relationships/interactions, and any grounded aliases or names heard. Prefer visible, spoken, OCR, or credited evidence over guesses.

fields.name: relationships
fields.description: Each relationship starting FROM this entity, output exactly as `dst_name;dst_type;rel_type;desc` (empty array if none). dst_name / dst_type must copy another emitted entity verbatim. rel_type is `actor_portrays_character` (person -> character only; desc must be `other`) or `has_social_relationship` (same type only). desc must be exactly one of family / friend / colleague / acquaintance / rival / other. Do not add evidence text, labels, colons, extra semicolons, or any value outside the enum in this field. Put relationship evidence in the entity description instead. Emit only clearly evidenced ties, not mere co-presence; emit symmetric social ties once.

fields.name: best_thumbnail
fields.description: Best, clearest, most front-facing frame of the entity. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

fields.name: second_best_thumbnail
fields.description: A frame as different as possible from best_thumbnail — a different time, ideally a different shot, and a different pose / angle / expression. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

fields.name: third_best_thumbnail
fields.description: A frame distinct from and far in time from both other thumbnails, so the three span the entity's varied appearances. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. fields.name: description
fields.description: Describe this shot in rich, verbose detail — what happens in the story here (intent, action, consequence) and every visual and non-visual detail: the interactions, objects, setting, on-screen text, and a verbatim transcription of any speech (with the speaker when certain). Refer to each person or character by the EXACT same name you give it in the `entities` field. Write enough that the shot stands on its own and, read in order, the shots reconstruct the whole story.

fields.name: entities
fields.description: Each entity evidenced in this shot, output exactly as `name;entity_type;description`. Include only entities visible, audible, or OCR-referenced in this shot: foreground or speaking persons/characters, important addressed or interacted-with people/characters, and entities needed to understand the action, conflict, portrayal, or relationship; also central places, objects, concepts, or things when they drive the shot's meaning. entity_type is one of person / character / place / object / concept / thing, lowercase. Use exactly two semicolons as separators; do not put semicolons inside name, entity_type, or description (use commas or dashes). For the `name` element (person/character): Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. Decide from all available context, not this shot alone. The name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'). **Use that entity's exact same name in every shot.** For the `description` element: Describe the entity for IDENTIFICATION — so that, from descriptions alone, one can tell which mentions across shots are the SAME person. Write SEVERAL sentences and pack in AS MANY stable identifying properties as you can: any name or alias heard, role, gender, approximate age, ethnicity, hair (color/length/style), facial features, build/height, distinctive marks, and especially their relationship or recurring interaction with other identified entities (e.g. 'the protagonist's mother', 'the man who repeatedly argues with the bald guard'), which strongly anchors identity. Do NOT describe clothing, location, or momentary physical action — they change and do not help identification.

metadata.title: Southampton vs Liverpool - Match Highlights
metadata.description: In this segment of the Premier League match between Southampton and Liverpool, with the score at 1-0 to Southampton, the play is dynamic and contested. The sequence begins with a close-up of Southampton's new midfield man, who is praised for his energy and decent run. The action shifts to a wide view of the pitch where Southampton attempts to build an attack, but the ball is released too slowly. A significant moment occurs when Sadio Mané, playing against his former club, is involved in a play that results in a foul, leading to a free kick for Liverpool. The camera captures the referee's decision and the subsequent setup for the free kick. Liverpool's Alisson Becker restarts play from his own half, and the ball is circulated among the Liverpool players, including Henderson, Firmino, Salah, and Oxlade-Chamberlain, as they attempt to break down Southampton's defense. The segment also highlights a moment of concern for Southampton's Alcapades, who is one yellow card away from suspension. The play continues with a free kick for Southampton, taken by Paul Prouse, which is deflected and leads to a scramble in the box. The segment concludes with a replay of a foul on Bednarek, with commentators debating whether he was propelled to the ground or went down on his own.

axis_descriptions.entity: Extract every important person/character in the range once each, merging recurrences into one canonical entry. Include foreground people/characters, speakers, addressed people/characters, partially visible but important interactors, and entities needed to understand actions, conflicts, portrayals, or relationships. Do not skip an unknown-name entity if it is distinguishable or narratively important. Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. Appearance segmentation — entities are extracted segment by segment, so a lead who recurs in every segment can crowd out the people around them. Treat each NEW person/character's first appearance as the start of a new entity segment. When several people appear at once, split that window into shorter appearance intervals and emit EVERY person in it — including secondary, briefly-seen, or interacting-but-not-central people — not only the most prominent one.
axis_descriptions.shot: This is the 7th of 10 parts of the video, and it is 296 seconds long. Segment this part into granular, contiguous shots using visual and audio cues: cuts, major camera-motion changes, event/action beats, dialogue or speaker turns, subject/framing/setting changes, and entity entries/exits. When unsure, prefer shorter coherent shots. Cover this part end to end with no gaps: the FIRST shot must start at 0 and the LAST shot must end at 296 (the full length of this part). Do not omit or trim static or silent spans at the head or tail.
axis_descriptions.video: This is the 7th of 10 parts of the video. Emit exactly ONE segment covering this whole part, using chunk-relative times: `start_time=0`, `end_time=296`.

fields.name: canonical_name
fields.description: Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. These entities are later merged across videos by matching name, so the name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'), which would wrongly merge unrelated people. One name per entity; the description elaborates further.

fields.name: entity_type
fields.description: Whether this entity is a person or a character.

fields.name: description
fields.description: Identifying description of this entity. Elaborate the entity to support its name and future cross-video merging: stable appearance, role, key relationships/interactions, and any grounded aliases or names heard. Prefer visible, spoken, OCR, or credited evidence over guesses.

fields.name: relationships
fields.description: Each relationship starting FROM this entity, output exactly as `dst_name;dst_type;rel_type;desc` (empty array if none). dst_name / dst_type must copy another emitted entity verbatim. rel_type is `actor_portrays_character` (person -> character only; desc must be `other`) or `has_social_relationship` (same type only). desc must be exactly one of family / friend / colleague / acquaintance / rival / other. Do not add evidence text, labels, colons, extra semicolons, or any value outside the enum in this field. Put relationship evidence in the entity description instead. Emit only clearly evidenced ties, not mere co-presence; emit symmetric social ties once.

fields.name: best_thumbnail
fields.description: Best, clearest, most front-facing frame of the entity. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

fields.name: second_best_thumbnail
fields.description: A frame as different as possible from best_thumbnail — a different time, ideally a different shot, and a different pose / angle / expression. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

fields.name: third_best_thumbnail
fields.description: A frame distinct from and far in time from both other thumbnails, so the three span the entity's varied appearances. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

fields.name: description
fields.description: Describe this shot in rich, verbose detail — what happens in the story here (intent, action, consequence) and every visual and non-visual detail: the interactions, objects, setting, on-screen text, and a verbatim transcription of any speech (with the speaker when certain). Refer to each person or character by the EXACT same name you give it in the `entities` field. Write enough that the shot stands on its own and, read in order, the shots reconstruct the whole story.

fields.name: shot_type
fields.description: The type of the shot.

fields.name: camera_motion
fields.description: The camera motion of the shot.

fields.name: camera_angle
fields.description: The camera angle of the shot.

fields.name: entities
fields.description: Each entity evidenced in this shot, output exactly as `name;entity_type;description`. Include only entities visible, audible, or OCR-referenced in this shot: foreground or speaking persons/characters, important addressed or interacted-with people/characters, and entities needed to understand the action, conflict, portrayal, or relationship; also central places, objects, concepts, or things when they drive the shot's meaning. entity_type is one of person / character / place / object / concept / thing, lowercase. Use exactly two semicolons as separators; do not put semicolons inside name, entity_type, or description (use commas or dashes). For the `name` element (person/character): Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. Decide from all available context, not this shot alone. The name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'). **Use that entity's exact same name in every shot.** For the `description` element: Describe the entity for IDENTIFICATION — so that, from descriptions alone, one can tell which mentions across shots are the SAME person. Write SEVERAL sentences and pack in AS MANY stable identifying properties as you can: any name or alias heard, role, gender, approximate age, ethnicity, hair (color/length/style), facial features, build/height, distinctive marks, and especially their relationship or recurring interaction with other identified entities (e.g. 'the protagonist's mother', 'the man who repeatedly argues with the bald guard'), which strongly anchors identity. Do NOT describe clothing, location, or momentary physical action — they change and do not help identification.

segment_definitions.description: This is the 7th of 10 parts of the video. Emit exactly ONE segment covering this whole part, using chunk-relative times: `start_time=0`, `end_time=296`.

fields.name: title
fields.description: Title of the whole video; best guess from in-chunk evidence (titles / credits / dialogue).

fields.name: description
fields.description: Narrate this chunk's story in full using only evidence from the emitted shot segments — what happens and why: the plot progression, the characters who drive it and their motivations, the conflicts, turns, and consequences. Hold as much grounded information as possible; do not introduce unsupported facts. Integrate events, objects, story context, visual detail, and audio/OCR/transcription facts into one account: names, relationships, intentions, conflicts, interaction evidence, and production cues like 'Action!'/'Cut!' when evidenced. Transcribe spoken lines verbatim when audible; do not assert an uncertain speaker.

metadata.title: Southampton vs Liverpool: Djenepo Injury and Substitution
metadata.description: In a Premier League match between Southampton and Liverpool, with the score at 1-0 to Southampton, Moussa Djenepo (number 12) suffers a hamstring injury after a challenge by Trent Alexander-Arnold. Replays show Djenepo falling and clutching his leg. Medical staff attend to him on the field, and he is subsequently substituted off for Nathan Tella (number 23). The substitution is shown with the electronic board displaying the player numbers. Djenepo walks off the pitch with medical staff, looking dejected, and is consoled by his manager, Ralph Hasenhüttl. The game resumes with Liverpool in possession, and Sadio Mané (number 10) takes a shot that goes wide of the goal. The segment concludes with a Sky Sports recap of the match highlights.

gemini_result.title: Southampton vs Liverpool - Premier League Match
gemini_result.description: During a Premier League match between Southampton and Liverpool, Southampton player Moussa Djenepo suffers a hamstring injury after a challenge by Trent Alexander-Arnold. He receives medical attention on the pitch and is eventually substituted by Nathan Tella. Djenepo is visibly upset as he leaves the field, comforted by his manager Ralph Hasenhüttl. Later in the clip, Liverpool builds an attack, resulting in a missed header opportunity by Sadio Mané from a cross by Thiago Alcantara.

axis_descriptions.entity: Extract every important person/character in the range once each, merging recurrences into one canonical entry. Include foreground people/characters, speakers, addressed people/characters, partially visible but important interactors, and entities needed to understand actions, conflicts, portrayals, or relationships. Do not skip an unknown-name entity if it is distinguishable or narratively important. Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. Appearance segmentation — entities are extracted segment by segment, so a lead who recurs in every segment can crowd out the people around them. Treat each NEW person/character's first appearance as the start of a new entity segment. When several people appear at once, split that window into shorter appearance intervals and emit EVERY person in it — including secondary, briefly-seen, or interacting-but-not-central people — not only the most prominent one.

Do not omit or trim static or silent spans at the head or tail.
axis_descriptions.video: This is the 8th of 10 parts of the video. Emit exactly ONE segment covering this whole part, using chunk-relative times: `start_time=0`, `end_time=296`.

fields.name: canonical_name
fields.description: Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. These entities are later merged across videos by matching name, so the name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'), which would wrongly merge unrelated people. One name per entity; the description elaborates further.

fields.name: entity_type
fields.description: Whether this entity is a person or a character.

fields.name: description
fields.description: Identifying description of this entity. Elaborate the entity to support its name and future cross-video merging: stable appearance, role, key relationships/interactions, and any grounded aliases or names heard. Prefer visible, spoken, OCR, or credited evidence over guesses.

fields.name: relationships
fields.description: Each relationship starting FROM this entity, output exactly as `dst_name;dst_type;rel_type;desc` (empty array if none). dst_name / dst_type must copy another emitted entity verbatim. rel_type is `actor_portrays_character` (person -> character only; desc must be `other`) or `has_social_relationship` (same type only). desc must be exactly one of family / friend / colleague / acquaintance / rival / other. Do not add evidence text, labels, colons, extra semicolons, or any value outside the enum in this field. Put relationship evidence in the entity description instead. Emit only clearly evidenced ties, not mere co-presence; emit symmetric social ties once.

fields.name: best_thumbnail
fields.description: Best, clearest, most front-facing frame of the entity. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

fields.name: second_best_thumbnail
fields.description: A frame as different as possible from best_thumbnail — a different time, ideally a different shot, and a different pose / angle / expression. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

fields.name: third_best_thumbnail
fields.description: A frame distinct from and far in time from both other thumbnails, so the three span the entity's varied appearances. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. fields.name: description
fields.description: Describe this shot in rich, verbose detail — what happens in the story here (intent, action, consequence) and every visual and non-visual detail: the interactions, objects, setting, on-screen text, and a verbatim transcription of any speech (with the speaker when certain). Refer to each person or character by the EXACT same name you give it in the `entities` field. Write enough that the shot stands on its own and, read in order, the shots reconstruct the whole story.

fields.name: entities
fields.description: Each entity evidenced in this shot, output exactly as `name;entity_type;description`. Include only entities visible, audible, or OCR-referenced in this shot: foreground or speaking persons/characters, important addressed or interacted-with people/characters, and entities needed to understand the action, conflict, portrayal, or relationship; also central places, objects, concepts, or things when they drive the shot's meaning. entity_type is one of person / character / place / object / concept / thing, lowercase. Use exactly two semicolons as separators; do not put semicolons inside name, entity_type, or description (use commas or dashes). For the `name` element (person/character): Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. Decide from all available context, not this shot alone. The name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'). **Use that entity's exact same name in every shot.** For the `description` element: Describe the entity for IDENTIFICATION — so that, from descriptions alone, one can tell which mentions across shots are the SAME person. Write SEVERAL sentences and pack in AS MANY stable identifying properties as you can: any name or alias heard, role, gender, approximate age, ethnicity, hair (color/length/style), facial features, build/height, distinctive marks, and especially their relationship or recurring interaction with other identified entities (e.g. 'the protagonist's mother', 'the man who repeatedly argues with the bald guard'), which strongly anchors identity. Do NOT describe clothing, location, or momentary physical action — they change and do not help identification.

fields.name: description
fields.description: Narrate this chunk's story in full using only evidence from the emitted shot segments — what happens and why: the plot progression, the characters who drive it and their motivations, the conflicts, turns, and consequences. Hold as much grounded information as possible; do not introduce unsupported facts. Integrate events, objects, story context, visual detail, and audio/OCR/transcription facts into one account: names, relationships, intentions, conflicts, interaction evidence, and production cues like 'Action!'/'Cut!' when evidenced. Transcribe spoken lines verbatim when audible; do not assert an uncertain speaker.

metadata.title: Southampton vs Liverpool - Penalty Decision and Match Highlights
metadata.description: In this segment of the Premier League match between Southampton and Liverpool, with the score at 1-0 to Southampton, the play is dominated by Liverpool's attempts to equalize. The action begins with Southampton's Fraser Forster making a save and distributing the ball. Liverpool maintains possession, with players like Alexander-Arnold and Salah involved in the buildup. A significant moment occurs when Mohamed Salah is fouled by Ryan Bertrand inside the penalty area. The referee consults VAR, and after a brief review, a penalty is awarded to Liverpool. The segment includes replays of the foul and the referee's decision-making process. Following the penalty decision, Liverpool takes a free kick, which is cleared by the Southampton defense. The clip concludes with Liverpool continuing to press forward, looking for another opportunity to score.

axis_descriptions.entity: Extract every important person/character in the range once each, merging recurrences into one canonical entry. Include foreground people/characters, speakers, addressed people/characters, partially visible but important interactors, and entities needed to understand actions, conflicts, portrayals, or relationships. Do not skip an unknown-name entity if it is distinguishable or narratively important. Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. Appearance segmentation — entities are extracted segment by segment, so a lead who recurs in every segment can crowd out the people around them. Treat each NEW person/character's first appearance as the start of a new entity segment. When several people appear at once, split that window into shorter appearance intervals and emit EVERY person in it — including secondary, briefly-seen, or interacting-but-not-central people — not only the most prominent one.

Segment this part into granular, contiguous shots using visual and audio cues: cuts, major camera-motion changes, event/action beats, dialogue or speaker turns, subject/framing/setting changes, and entity entries/exits. When unsure, prefer shorter coherent shots. Cover this part end to end with no gaps: the FIRST shot must start at 0 and the LAST shot must end at 297 (the full length of this part). Do not omit or trim static or silent spans at the head or tail.
axis_descriptions.video: This is the 9th of 10 parts of the video. Emit exactly ONE segment covering this whole part, using chunk-relative times: `start_time=0`, `end_time=297`.

fields.name: canonical_name
fields.description: Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. These entities are later merged across videos by matching name, so the name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'), which would wrongly merge unrelated people. One name per entity; the description elaborates further.

fields.name: entity_type
fields.description: Whether this entity is a person or a character.

fields.name: description
fields.description: Identifying description of this entity. Elaborate the entity to support its name and future cross-video merging: stable appearance, role, key relationships/interactions, and any grounded aliases or names heard. Prefer visible, spoken, OCR, or credited evidence over guesses.

fields.name: relationships
fields.description: Each relationship starting FROM this entity, output exactly as `dst_name;dst_type;rel_type;desc` (empty array if none). dst_name / dst_type must copy another emitted entity verbatim. rel_type is `actor_portrays_character` (person -> character only; desc must be `other`) or `has_social_relationship` (same type only). desc must be exactly one of family / friend / colleague / acquaintance / rival / other. Do not add evidence text, labels, colons, extra semicolons, or any value outside the enum in this field. Put relationship evidence in the entity description instead. Emit only clearly evidenced ties, not mere co-presence; emit symmetric social ties once.

fields.name: best_thumbnail
fields.description: Best, clearest, most front-facing frame of the entity. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. fields.name: second_best_thumbnail

fields.name: third_best_thumbnail

Use exactly two semicolons as separators; do not put semicolons inside name, entity_type, or description (use commas or dashes). For the `name` element (person/character): Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. Decide from all available context, not this shot alone. The name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'). **Use that entity's exact same name in every shot.** For the `description` element: Describe the entity for IDENTIFICATION — so that, from descriptions alone, one can tell which mentions across shots are the SAME person. Write SEVERAL sentences and pack in AS MANY stable identifying properties as you can: any name or alias heard, role, gender, approximate age, ethnicity, hair (color/length/style), facial features, build/height, distinctive marks, and especially their relationship or recurring interaction with other identified entities (e.g. 'the protagonist's mother', 'the man who repeatedly argues with the bald guard'), which strongly anchors identity. Do NOT describe clothing, location, or momentary physical action — they change and do not help identification.

metadata.title: Southampton vs Liverpool - First Half Highlights
metadata.description: In this segment of the Premier League match between Southampton and Liverpool, with the score at 1-0 to Southampton, the action is intense. At 00:31, Southampton's Tella receives a pass inside the Liverpool penalty area and takes a shot that is saved by the goalkeeper, leading to Tella's visible frustration. The game continues with Liverpool attempting to build attacks, but Southampton's defense remains solid. A significant moment occurs around 02:04 when Liverpool's Robertson crosses the ball into the box, leading to a scramble where Southampton's goalkeeper, Forster, collides with his own defender, Ryan Bertrand, causing both to fall. Replays confirm the collision was accidental. Later, at 02:51, Tella has another chance, curling a shot that narrowly misses the target, prompting a reaction of disbelief from manager Ralph Hasenhüttl. The segment concludes with Southampton preparing for a corner kick taken by James Ward-Prowse as the first half nears its end.

The clip features several attacking sequences, including a missed shot by Nathan Tella, a penalty shout involving Mohamed Salah and Fraser Forster, a missed shot by Jordan Henderson, and a counter-attack leading to a chance for Danny Ings. The footage includes live action, replays, and player/manager reactions.

axis_descriptions.entity: Extract every important person/character in the range once each, merging recurrences into one canonical entry. Include foreground people/characters, speakers, addressed people/characters, partially visible but important interactors, and entities needed to understand actions, conflicts, portrayals, or relationships. Do not skip an unknown-name entity if it is distinguishable or narratively important. Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. Appearance segmentation — entities are extracted segment by segment, so a lead who recurs in every segment can crowd out the people around them. Treat each NEW person/character's first appearance as the start of a new entity segment. When several people appear at once, split that window into shorter appearance intervals and emit EVERY person in it — including secondary, briefly-seen, or interacting-but-not-central people — not only the most prominent one.
axis_descriptions.shot: This is the 10th of 10 parts of the video, and it is 296 seconds long. Segment this part into granular, contiguous shots using visual and audio cues: cuts, major camera-motion changes, event/action beats, dialogue or speaker turns, subject/framing/setting changes, and entity entries/exits. When unsure, prefer shorter coherent shots. Cover this part end to end with no gaps: the FIRST shot must start at 0 and the LAST shot must end at 296 (the full length of this part). Do not omit or trim static or silent spans at the head or tail.
axis_descriptions.video: This is the 10th of 10 parts of the video. Emit exactly ONE segment covering this whole part, using chunk-relative times: `start_time=0`, `end_time=296`.

segment_definitions.description: Extract every important person/character in the range once each, merging recurrences into one canonical entry. Include foreground people/characters, speakers, addressed people/characters, partially visible but important interactors, and entities needed to understand actions, conflicts, portrayals, or relationships. Do not skip an unknown-name entity if it is distinguishable or narratively important. Skip background extras with no interaction or story function. person = real individual, character = fictional/performed role; if both a real person and their portrayed character are identifiable, emit them separately. Appearance segmentation — entities are extracted segment by segment, so a lead who recurs in every segment can crowd out the people around them. Treat each NEW person/character's first appearance as the start of a new entity segment. When several people appear at once, split that window into shorter appearance intervals and emit EVERY person in it — including secondary, briefly-seen, or interacting-but-not-central people — not only the most prominent one.

fields.name: canonical_name
fields.description: Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. These entities are later merged across videos by matching name, so the name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'), which would wrongly merge unrelated people. One name per entity; the description elaborates further.

fields.name: entity_type
fields.description: Whether this entity is a person or a character.

fields.name: description
fields.description: Identifying description of this entity. Elaborate the entity to support its name and future cross-video merging: stable appearance, role, key relationships/interactions, and any grounded aliases or names heard. Prefer visible, spoken, OCR, or credited evidence over guesses.

fields.name: relationships
fields.description: Each relationship starting FROM this entity, output exactly as `dst_name;dst_type;rel_type;desc` (empty array if none). dst_name / dst_type must copy another emitted entity verbatim. rel_type is `actor_portrays_character` (person -> character only; desc must be `other`) or `has_social_relationship` (same type only). desc must be exactly one of family / friend / colleague / acquaintance / rival / other. Do not add evidence text, labels, colons, extra semicolons, or any value outside the enum in this field. Put relationship evidence in the entity description instead. Emit only clearly evidenced ties, not mere co-presence; emit symmetric social ties once.

fields.name: best_thumbnail
fields.description: Best, clearest, most front-facing frame of the entity. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

fields.name: second_best_thumbnail
fields.description: A frame as different as possible from best_thumbnail — a different time, ideally a different shot, and a different pose / angle / expression. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

fields.name: third_best_thumbnail
fields.description: A frame distinct from and far in time from both other thumbnails, so the three span the entity's varied appearances. Output a list of 5 numbers `[time_sec, y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max]`: time_sec is seconds from the chunk start; the box is normalized to [0,1000] (top-left, bottom-right) and tightly encloses the entity. Output [] if not clearly visible.

Segment this part into granular, contiguous shots using visual and audio cues: cuts, major camera-motion changes, event/action beats, dialogue or speaker turns, subject/framing/setting changes, and entity entries/exits. When unsure, prefer shorter coherent shots. Cover this part end to end with no gaps: the FIRST shot must start at 0 and the LAST shot must end at 296 (the full length of this part). Do not omit or trim static or silent spans at the head or tail.

fields.name: description
fields.description: Describe this shot in rich, verbose detail — what happens in the story here (intent, action, consequence) and every visual and non-visual detail: the interactions, objects, setting, on-screen text, and a verbatim transcription of any speech (with the speaker when certain). Refer to each person or character by the EXACT same name you give it in the `entities` field. Write enough that the shot stands on its own and, read in order, the shots reconstruct the whole story.

fields.name: shot_type
fields.description: The type of the shot.

fields.name: camera_motion
fields.description: The camera motion of the shot.

fields.name: camera_angle
fields.description: The camera angle of the shot.

fields.name: entities
fields.description: Each entity evidenced in this shot, output exactly as `name;entity_type;description`. Include only entities visible, audible, or OCR-referenced in this shot: foreground or speaking persons/characters, important addressed or interacted-with people/characters, and entities needed to understand the action, conflict, portrayal, or relationship; also central places, objects, concepts, or things when they drive the shot's meaning. entity_type is one of person / character / place / object / concept / thing, lowercase. Use exactly two semicolons as separators; do not put semicolons inside name, entity_type, or description (use commas or dashes). For the `name` element (person/character): Name, in order of preference: (1) the entity's full name (a real person's real name, or a character's in-story name); (2) a name spoken or shown on screen; (3) a descriptive label built from stable, distinguishing traits (e.g. 'bald older guard', 'red-haired teen') — avoid unstable traits like clothing. Decide from all available context, not this shot alone. The name must be specific enough that two DIFFERENT people never collide on the same name: never a bare generic ('man', 'woman', 'person'). **Use that entity's exact same name in every shot.** For the `description` element: Describe the entity for IDENTIFICATION — so that, from descriptions alone, one can tell which mentions across shots are the SAME person. Write SEVERAL sentences and pack in AS MANY stable identifying properties as you can: any name or alias heard, role, gender, approximate age, ethnicity, hair (color/length/style), facial features, build/height, distinctive marks, and especially their relationship or recurring interaction with other identified entities (e.g. 'the protagonist's mother', 'the man who repeatedly argues with the bald guard'), which strongly anchors identity. Do NOT describe clothing, location, or momentary physical action — they change and do not help identification.

segment_definitions.description: This is the 10th of 10 parts of the video. Emit exactly ONE segment covering this whole part, using chunk-relative times: `start_time=0`, `end_time=296`.

fields.name: title
fields.description: Title of the whole video; best guess from in-chunk evidence (titles / credits / dialogue).

fields.name: description
fields.description: Narrate this chunk's story in full using only evidence from the emitted shot segments — what happens and why: the plot progression, the characters who drive it and their motivations, the conflicts, turns, and consequences. Hold as much grounded information as possible; do not introduce unsupported facts. Integrate events, objects, story context, visual detail, and audio/OCR/transcription facts into one account: names, relationships, intentions, conflicts, interaction evidence, and production cues like 'Action!'/'Cut!' when evidenced. Transcribe spoken lines verbatim when audible; do not assert an uncertain speaker.

fields.name: work
fields.description: Optional work title inferred from this chunk (for root candidate materialization).

metadata.title: Southampton vs Liverpool - First Half Conclusion
metadata.description: In the final minutes of the first half, Liverpool presses for an equalizer against Southampton, who lead 1-0. Liverpool's attack intensifies with quick passing and a header by Mohamed Salah that is deflected by Ryan Bertrand. A controversial moment occurs when Liverpool is awarded a corner after a cross by Robertson, despite Southampton's protests that the ball went out for a throw-in. The referee and linesman confirm the corner, but Liverpool fails to capitalize. As the half concludes, the players walk off the pitch, with Danny Ings and Mohamed Salah sharing a brief, friendly exchange. The commentators note Southampton's determined defense and Liverpool's need to improve in the second half.

gemini_result.title: Southampton vs Liverpool - Premier League First Half Conclusion
gemini_result.description: The final minutes of the first half of a Premier League match between Southampton and Liverpool at St Mary's Stadium. Southampton leads 1-0. Liverpool dominates possession and creates several chances, including headers by Mohamed Salah and Roberto Firmino, but fails to score. The half concludes with the referee blowing the whistle, and players and managers heading to the dressing rooms.
